target.name: XMOS
target.founder: Ali Dixon, James Foster, Noel Hurley, David May, Hitesh Mehta

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 10 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
XMOS is a growth-stage semiconductor company that has raised £200M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of 11 peers across Chips & Semiconductors: 3 public companies (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private firms including Cerebras, SambaNova Systems, PsiQuantum, Etched, and Tenstorrent.

The peer-median method is not applicable here. Despite identifying 10 same-sector peers, filtering by stage, adjacent stage, or valuation tier yielded fewer than three datapoints in any narrow bucket. Applying an all-sector median would introduce substantial noise given the heterogeneity within semiconductor subsectors and business models. The comp set is therefore insufficient to support a reliable median-multiple triangulation.

Without executable triangulation methods, a quantitative valuation range cannot be responsibly derived from the available data. The analysis highlights a material constraint: XMOS occupies a discrete strategic and technical niche within semiconductors, and comparable growth-stage companies with sufficient financial transparency remain sparse. To proceed, additional data on the private peer set's revenue multiples, gross margins, or recent funding rounds would be required. Public comps (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) operate at vastly different scales and market structures, limiting their applicability. Key uncertainties include XMOS's revenue run rate, unit economics, and end-market traction relative to its peer cohort.
